results.possibleIdentity: David Paris (born c. 1940) is a retired aerospace engineer and technical collaborator who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, a period coinciding with the height of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. He is notably documented as a "creative co-conspirator" of the sculptor Allan Tetkowski during Tetkowski's "Ashland years" in Buffalo, New York, where they collaborated on artistic projects. Professionally, Paris was an engineer at Grumman in Bethpage, Long Island, an organization whose technical staff frequently engaged with E.A.T. projects like the Pepsi-Cola Pavilion (1970). In the interdisciplinary spirit of E.A.T., engineers like Paris were often integral participants in the creation of technologically sophisticated art, bridging the gap between scientific expertise and artistic vision. Paris also held leadership roles in professional engineering organizations, serving as the Chairman of the American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics (AIAA) Long Island Section. In his retirement, he moved into the performing arts as a background actor for television series such as 'Boardwalk Empire' and 'Pan Am'.

results.possibleIdentity: John "Nelson" Richardson (1947–2005) was an American artist, graphic designer, and educator active from the late 1960s through the early 2000s. Born in Santa Monica, California, he was a graduate of Humboldt State University and a veteran of the Vietnam War. His artistic practice was characterized by an interest in the intersection of art and technology, which aligns with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Professionally, he served as the Art Director and Designer for KBAK-TV (Channel 17) in Bakersfield, California, where he was an early practitioner of computer-generated graphics for television in the 1970s and 1980s. As a painter, his work often employed optical illusions, abstract structures, and pointillist techniques to explore depth perception and landscape. Notable pieces include "When Values Meet" (1975) and "No. 4 (Searchlight Illusion)." He was also a longtime professor at Bakersfield College, where he developed and taught the computer graphic arts program starting in the mid-1980s. In 1991, he created "Christo Reaction," a multi-panel acrylic work that paid homage to Christo and Jeanne-Claude's Land Art installation "The Umbrellas."

In 1971, he was present at Villa Nellcôte in France during the recording of the Rolling Stones' album 'Exile on Main St.', where he was described as a sound and light engineer who brought custom-made rockets/fireworks to the sessions. Byall studied art at the University of California, Santa Barbara (UCSB) in the early 1970s and later transition into the film industry, where he worked as a visual effects grip and electrician on major productions including 'Terminator 2: Judgment Day' (1991) and 'Star Trek Nemesis' (2002). His background in both engineering and fine arts aligns with the collaborative mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

results.possibleIdentity: Cristos Gianakos (also known as Cris Gianakos) is a prominent American postminimalist artist, sculptor, and installation artist born on January 4, 1934, in New York City. He is particularly recognized for his 'Rampworks'—large-scale, site-specific ramp sculptures made from raw materials like wood and steel that explore geometric forms and architectural space. Gianakos began his career in the late 1960s, initially working with cast polyester resin and participating in the New York avant-garde scene. His early activities included performances at the Judson Dance Theater and ephemeral public installations, such as his 1969 'White Powder Pieces' in Central Park. A notable figure in the development of conceptual art logistics, he designed the 'Artist's Reserved Rights and Transfer Sales Agreement' in 1971 in collaboration with Seth Siegelaub. Since 1963, he has been a faculty member at the School of Visual Arts (SVA) in New York. His work is held in major international collections, including the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Whitney Museum of American Art, the Brooklyn Museum, and the Moderna Museet in Stockholm.

results.possibleIdentity: Richard L. Huggins (active 1950s–1970s) was a prominent American industrial and silver designer who served as the Director of Design for the Gorham Manufacturing Company starting in 1962. He is best known for designing the presentation silver service for the USS Long Beach (the first nuclear-powered cruiser) in 1961. His work was hailed as the "essence of the atomic age captured in sterling silver," utilizing "vital forms" such as atomic models with electron paths for finials and biomorphic shapes inspired by mushroom clouds. His designs are considered significant examples of the intersection between art, science, and technology during the Cold War era. His work has been featured in major exhibitions, including "Modernism in American Silver" at the Dallas Museum of Art and "Vital Forms: American Art and Design in the Atomic Age, 1940-1960" at the Brooklyn Museum of Art. His career coincides with the period of activity for the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, and his design philosophy aligned with their goal of integrating artistic expression with scientific and technological advancement.

Richard Huggins is a contemporary academic and Senior Lecturer in Politics at Oxford Brookes University who has published extensively on the relationship between new technologies and political identity. He is the co-editor of the book 'New Media and Politics' (2001), which examines how digital transformations and electronic media affect the public sphere and citizenship. While primarily an academic, his work explores the 'aestheticization of social and political life' and the role of 'image' and 'mediatized cultures,' themes that overlap with the historical interests of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He was active in research and publication throughout the late 20th century.

results.possibleIdentity: Lazar Lee Mintz (1947–1988), also known as Lee Mintz, was a visual and performance artist and writer active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He was a core member of the Lavender Hill Commune, an intentional community in Newfield, New York, founded in 1973 that was primarily composed of artists, writers, and political activists. Mintz was involved in the countercultural movement and contributed to publications such as RFD (Radical Faerie Digest), where he wrote on themes of identity and community (e.g., "City/Country" in 1975). His work and life were part of the broader 1970s-era communal and artistic experimentation that sought to merge creative practice with alternative social structures.

Norbett L. Mintz (frequently cited as L. Mintz) was a psychologist and researcher active in the 1950s through the 1970s who specialized in the effects of aesthetic environments. He is best known for his 1956 collaborative study with Abraham Maslow, "Effects of Esthetic Surroundings," which examined how the visual quality of a room (beautiful vs. ugly) influenced human perception and well-being. This research became foundational to environmental psychology and was widely cited by architects and experimental artists in the 1960s and 1970s, including those associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), who were exploring immersive, technology-driven environmental art.

Lawrence Mintz (also known as Lawrence E. Mintz) is a scholar and historian of popular culture and performance art who became prominent in the early 1970s. He specialized in the study of stand-up comedy as a form of social and cultural mediation, theorizing it as a significant performance art in American history. While primarily an academic, his work involves documenting and analyzing the artistic techniques of performers and media artists during the late 1960s and 1970s, a period marked by the intersections of art, humor, and technology.

results.possibleIdentity: Daniel (Daniël) Weinberger is a Belgian performance and video artist who was active in the early-to-mid 1970s. He is recognized as part of the first generation of video art pioneers in Belgium, particularly associated with the Internationaal Cultureel Centrum (ICC) in Antwerp. His work often explored the intersection of the physical body and electronic media. One of his most notable performances, described in the context of 'Body Art' (1977), involved a bodybuilding act where he carried two television monitors displaying his own image, effectively 'lifting' his own electronic representation. He participated in significant experimental art events of the era, including 'Expmtl 5' (1974) in Knokke and the 'Artists Video Tapes' exhibition (1975) at the Palais des Beaux-Arts in Brussels, which featured other prominent avant-garde figures such as Joseph Beuys and Allan Kaprow. His name is mentioned in historical surveys of 'Experiments in Art and Technology' (E.A.T.) festivals and the development of participatory digital art.

David Weinberger (born 1950) is an American technologist, philosopher, and writer whose career began in the late 1960s and 1970s. He earned a PhD in philosophy from the University of Toronto in 1976 and is frequently cited in modern academic handbooks concerning 'Experiments in Art and Technology' (E.A.T.) due to his influential theories on networked knowledge and the impact of technology on society (most notably in his 2012 book 'Too Big to Know'). Early in his career, he worked at Bell Labs, the industrial research organization where E.A.T. was founded by engineers Billy Klüver and Fred Waldhauer. In 1976, he also contributed to creative media as a joke writer for a Woody Allen comic strip. He is currently a researcher at Harvard's metaLAB, which focuses on the intersection of art, technology, and the humanities, and he has been described as a 'prophet' for the collaborative and non-hierarchical values that E.A.T. originally promoted.

results.possibleIdentity: Sari Dienes (1898–1992) was a prolific Hungarian-born American experimental artist and a central figure in the mid-20th-century New York avant-garde. Often referred to as the 'doyenne of the American avant-garde,' Dienes is most famous for her large-scale 'sidewalk rubbings' (frottage) of urban textures like manhole covers and subway grates, a technique she pioneered in the early 1950s using ink and a medical material called Webril. Her work and philosophy, heavily influenced by Zen Buddhism, emphasized finding art in humble, found objects, directly influencing younger contemporaries such as Robert Rauschenberg and Jasper Johns. During the late 1960s and 1970s, Dienes remained highly active; she was a founding member of the feminist artist cooperative A.I.R. Gallery in 1972 and a frequent participant in Charlotte Moorman's Annual Avant Garde Festivals. She was also associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, with her proposals and correspondence preserved in the E.A.T. records at the Getty Research Institute. From 1961 until her death, she lived at the Gate Hill Cooperative ('The Land') in Stony Point, New York, alongside other notable avant-garde figures like John Cage and Merce Cunningham. Her diverse output included painting, sculpture, ceramics, textile design, performance art, and sound-art installations.

results.possibleIdentity: Julie Katz was an artist and photographer active in the late 1960s and early 1970s who was associated with the early video art movement and its intersection with technology. She is most notably recognized for co-designing the cover of the second issue of Radical Software (Volume I, Number 2, Fall 1970) alongside video artist Tom DeWitt. Radical Software was a pioneering journal that served as a key communication network for artists, engineers, and activists interested in video as an artistic medium and its social potential. Katz's work in this period is linked to the same creative milieu as the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.); her collaborator Tom DeWitt was a frequent associate of E.A.T. and contributed to major projects such as the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka. Archival materials and contributions from Katz, specifically her work for Radical Software, have been featured in major exhibitions exploring the history of electronic art, such as the Tate’s "Electric Dreams" exhibition.

results.possibleIdentity: John L. Chapman (born 1946) is a British artist known for his oil, acrylic, and gouache paintings of urban landscapes, townscapes, and transport scenes, including railways and maritime subjects. He began his career in the late 1960s and early 1970s, producing works that often evoke a sense of nostalgia for industrial and rural Britain. His art has been featured in commercial products such as Wedgwood fine bone china plates (e.g., 'The Wheelwright' and 'Life on the Farm' series). He is a well-exhibited painter with a career spanning over 50 years, and his works are frequently sold at major auction houses like Adam Partridge and Capes Dunn.

Laura H. Chapman (often cited as L. Chapman) is a highly influential American art educator and author whose career began in the late 1960s. She is widely recognized for her contributions to art curriculum development and the philosophy of art education. Her books, such as 'Approaches to Art in Education' (1978) and the K-12 textbook series 'Discover Art' (first published in 1985), are foundational texts in the field. Her research focuses on how children conceptualize art and the importance of structured art programs in schools. While primarily known as an educator and scholar, her name is the most prominent 'L. Chapman' appearing in artistic and educational literature from the 1970s.

Linn Chapman (also referred to as Linnie Chapman) was a central staff member of the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during its most active period in the late 1960s and early 1970s. She served as the Matching Service Coordinator and Executive Secretary, playing a critical role in facilitating collaborations between artists, engineers, and scientists by pairing them based on technical needs. She was involved in the organization's administration at their loft office in New York and was a key figure in managing requests for technical assistance from thousands of members worldwide. While primarily documented as an administrative coordinator, E.A.T. staff were deeply integrated into the collaborative projects of the era.

results.possibleIdentity: Stephanie Evanitsky is a New York-based choreographer, dancer, and artist who is widely recognized as a pioneer and founder of "Aerial Dance." In 1969, she founded the Multigravitational Experiment Group (also known as the Multigravitational Group), an experimental dance company that performed off the ground using a self-standing 16-foot high aluminum scaffold. Her work, which she termed "Aerodance," utilized various apparatuses such as elastic tightropes, trapezes, rope webs, clear plastic sacks, and pulleys to create the illusion of movement in zero gravity and explore the intersection of dance, sculpture, and technology. She was active throughout the late 1960s and 1970s, with notable performances at the O.K. Harris Gallery (1969) and the Museum of Modern Art (1973). In 1974, she was awarded a Guggenheim Fellowship for Choreography. Her work is documented in the Jerome Robbins Papers at the New York Public Library, where her records are archived alongside other experimental organizations from the same era, including Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

results.possibleIdentity: Alice Strauss was a key administrative and editorial figure associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), the influential organization co-founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg in 1966. Active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, she served as the Managing Editor for E.A.T.'s periodical, Techne (published in 1969), and the E.A.T. Directory of Artists and Engineers. Her role was central to the organization's mission of fostering collaboration between the arts and scientific/technological communities, as she helped document and coordinate projects involving hundreds of artists and technical professionals. She was also part of the extensive team that worked on the E.A.T.-designed Pepsi Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, where she participated in the complex logistics and documentation of the project.

results.possibleIdentity: Oliver Gordon Selfridge (1926–2008) was a mathematician and computer scientist who is primarily recognized as a pioneer in Artificial Intelligence (AI) and the 'Father of Machine Perception.' Although a scientist by profession, his historical identity in the art world stems from his active participation in the 'Art and Technology' movement of the late 1960s and early 1970s. During this period, he was a key associate of the prominent art theorist and artist Jack Burnham. Selfridge worked with Burnham at the MIT Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS), where they explored the intersection of cybernetics, computer systems, and art. Their collaboration resulted in the influential essay 'The Aesthetics of Intelligent Systems' (1970), which investigated how computer logic and AI could inform artistic processes and systems aesthetics. Selfridge’s conceptual work on the 'Pandemonium' architecture—a model of parallel processing involving 'demons' that recognize patterns—was frequently referenced in early digital and systems-based art discourse. Additionally, Selfridge had a direct creative output as the author of several children's books, including 'All About Signals,' 'The Fingerprint,' and 'Trouble with Ticks.' He was a graduate of MIT under Norbert Wiener and spent a significant portion of his career at the MIT Lincoln Laboratory, Project MAC, and Bolt, Beranek and Newman (BBN).

results.possibleIdentity: Elizabeth "Betty" Voelker (born 1931) was an internationally recognized painter and colorist originally from Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. She earned her Bachelor of Fine Arts in 1953 from the Carnegie Institute of Technology (now Carnegie Mellon University), where she was a student of Samuel Rosenberg. In 1958, she moved to San Francisco, California. Her artistic practice focused on abstract nature paintings and formal abstracted collages. From 1972 to 1987, she served as a lecturer for various California museums and the University of California. Her work has been acquired by major institutions, including the National Museum of American Art in Washington D.C., the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, and the Carnegie Institute Art Museum. She received prestigious awards and residencies throughout her career, including three grants from the Pollock-Krasner Foundation, a Rockefeller Foundation Grant for the Bellagio Study and Conference Center in Italy, and residencies at the American Academy in Rome and the Djerassi Foundation.

Betty Voelker is a conceptual artist, born Betty Calvert in New Orleans, who was active under the name Betty Voelker until approximately 1977, after which she began working under the name Hera. In the 1970s, her work transitioned into the exploration of "tent forms" and environmental constructions. Notable works created during this period include "Sweet Skirt" (1977), a large taffeta structure containing objects related to womanhood, and "Death Singer," both of which were featured in the "Locations" exhibition at the Rose Art Museum at Brandeis University in 1977. Her work is often associated with feminist art collectives and social/political documentation, such as the West East Bag (W.E.B.) and the Political Art Documentation/Distribution (PAD/D). Her artistic transition and focus on structural, conceptual environments align with the collaborative interests of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s and 1970s.

results.possibleIdentity: Eric Orr (1939–1998) was an American artist primarily active in Venice, California, from the mid-1960s until his death. He was a central figure in the Southern California Light and Space movement and was known for his conceptual, perceptual-based installations, sculptures, and performances. Orr frequently collaborated with other artists and engineers, aligning with the goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Between 1967 and 1971, he was part of the group of Californian artists who joined the E.A.T. initiative at the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA), where artists were paired with scientists and engineers to explore psychological and physical phenomena. His work often utilized unconventional and scientific materials, including dry ice, gas, fire, water, gold, volcanic ash, and even his own blood. In 1966, he co-founded the 'Rooms Company' with Judy Chicago and Lloyd Hamrol, through which they created the seminal work 'Feather Room' (1966). His own notable works from the late 1960s and early 1970s include 'Zero Mass' (1969), an immersive installation designed to create a total visual void, and 'Sound Tunnel' (1969). He later participated in the 'C Project,' a collaboration focused on holography that involved other major artists such as James Turrell and Larry Bell.

results.possibleIdentity: Joan Webster Price (born 1931) is an American abstract artist, sculptor, printmaker, and educator known for her work in geometric abstraction and abstract spatial dynamics. Born in Camden, New Jersey, and also known as Joan Diane Webster, she has been a long-standing figure in the New York art scene, serving as a Professor Emerita at the City College of New York (CCNY). Her artistic practice often features bold lines, geometric forms, and vibrant colors, and she is a member of the American Abstract Artists (AAA). Price's work is held in the permanent collections of prestigious institutions including the Whitney Museum of American Art, the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Smithsonian American Art Museum, the Phillips Collection, and the McNay Art Museum. She was active during the late 1960s and 1970s as both an artist and an educator, notably influencing other artists such as Ellen Lee Klein. Her interest in the intersection of art and technology is reflected in her involvement with 'Leonardo' (the journal of the International Society for the Arts, Sciences and Technology), where she and Herbert Price are frequently listed as donors and supporters. Notable works include 'Tower Fantasy' (1987) and 'To Mars and Beyond' (1997).
